destination.name: Sicily
destination.hero_image_credit: Photo by Bernd Dittrich on Unsplash
destination.hero_image_alt: Volcanic landscape on Mount Etna, Sicily, with terraced vineyards on black basalt slopes
destination.blurb: Italy's island wine frontier: ancient Zibibbo on Pantelleria, century-old Nerello on Etna's volcanic terraces, Cerasuolo di Vittoria DOCG from the sun-drenched south-east, and the legendary Marsala cellars of the west.
destination.tagline: Volcano, sea wind, and native varieties that change with every contrada
destination.overview: Sicily is Italy's most varied wine island. The active volcano Etna gives Europe's most exciting high-altitude terroir for Nerello Mascalese and Carricante. To the west, Marsala's historic fortified wine tradition is being reclaimed by artisan producers like Marco De Bartoli. In the south-east, Cerasuolo di Vittoria DOCG - Sicily's only DOCG - blends Nero d'Avola and Frappato. And on Pantelleria, Zibibbo raisins yield Passito di Pantelleria, one of Italy's greatest sweet wines. Sicily also holds over 30 DOC appellations from Faro near Messina to Menfi on the south-west coast.

index.title: Sicily Wine Region Guide | Cork & Curve
index.description: Explore Sicily's wine regions from Etna's volcanic Nerello terraces to Marsala's historic cellars, Pantelleria's Zibibbo passito and Cerasuolo di Vittoria DOCG.

vineyards.title: Sicily Vineyards and Wineries | Cork & Curve
vineyards.description: Discover 50+ Sicily wine estates: Frank Cornelissen and Benanti on Etna, COS in Vittoria, Donnafugata in Marsala, plus Pantelleria and the Aeolian Islands.

wines.title: Sicily Wines Catalog | Cork & Curve
wines.description: Browse Sicily's cuvees: Etna Nerello from volcanic old vines, Cerasuolo di Vittoria DOCG, Marsala riserva, Passito di Pantelleria and Nero d'Avola.

tasting-rooms.title: Sicily Tasting Rooms and Cellar Doors | Cork & Curve
tasting-rooms.description: Best wine tasting experiences in Sicily: Etna contrada tastings, Marsala historic cellars, Pantelleria passito experiences and Vittoria estate visits.

wine-bars.title: Sicily Wine Bars and Enoteche | Cork & Curve
wine-bars.description: Sicily's best wine bars and enoteche in Palermo, Catania, Taormina and Ragusa, pouring Etna DOC, Nero d'Avola, Marsala and Sicilian natural wines.

wine-restaurants.title: Sicily Wine Restaurants | Cork & Curve
wine-restaurants.description: Restaurants with serious Sicilian wine programs, from Michelin-rated estate kitchens on Etna to Palermo trattorias pairing Nero d'Avola with street food.

signature-wines.title: Iconic Sicily Wines | Cork & Curve
signature-wines.description: Sicily's iconic bottles: Cornelissen Magma, Donnafugata Ben Rye, COS Cerasuolo di Vittoria, Benanti Pietramarina, De Bartoli Vecchio Samperi and more.

signature-grapes.title: Sicily Signature Grape Varieties | Cork & Curve
signature-grapes.description: Sicily's canonical grape varieties from Nerello Mascalese on Etna to Nero d'Avola, Frappato, Grillo, Carricante, Zibibbo, Catarratto, Malvasia di Lipari and more.

neighborhoods.title: Sicily Wine Sub-Appellations | Cork & Curve
neighborhoods.description: Sicily's wine sub-zones: Etna DOC contrade, Cerasuolo di Vittoria DOCG, Marsala DOC, Pantelleria, Noto, Menfi, Faro, Malvasia delle Lipari and more.

wine-history.title: Sicily Wine History | Cork & Curve
wine-history.description: Sicily's 4,000-year wine history from Phoenician colonists to Greek settlers, Arab interlude, Marsala trade wines, and the modern Etna renaissance.

natural.title: Natural Wine in Sicily | Cork & Curve
natural.description: Sicily's natural wine revolution: COS amphora wines, Frank Cornelissen sulphur-free Etna reds, Arianna Occhipinti biodynamic Frappato and Serragghia Pantelleria.

faqs.question: What is Sicily's only DOCG wine?
faqs.answer: Cerasuolo di Vittoria DOCG, awarded in 2005, is Sicily's only wine with Denominazione di Origine Controllata e Garantita status. It is a blend of Nero d'Avola (minimum 50%) and Frappato (minimum 30%) from the Vittoria zone in the Ragusa, Catania and Caltanissetta provinces. Key producers include COS, Arianna Occhipinti and Valle dell'Acate.

faqs.question: What grapes grow on Mount Etna?
faqs.answer: Etna DOC is dominated by two red varieties - Nerello Mascalese (the primary grape for Etna Rosso and Etna Rosato) and Nerello Cappuccio (a blending component). For whites, Carricante is the primary variety for Etna Bianco and Etna Bianco Superiore from the Milo area. Catarratto and Minnella Bianca are also authorised. The volcanic basalt soils and high altitude (400-1,000 metres) give exceptional minerality and freshness.

faqs.question: What is Passito di Pantelleria?
faqs.answer: Passito di Pantelleria DOC is a sweet wine made from sun-dried Zibibbo (Moscato d'Alessandria) grapes on the volcanic island of Pantelleria, 70 kilometres from Tunisia. Vines are trained in the alberello pantesco (bush vine) system to resist the fierce African wind. Donnafugata Ben Rye and Marco De Bartoli Bukkuram are the most celebrated examples.

faqs.question: What is Marsala wine and which producers should I visit?
faqs.answer: Marsala DOC is a fortified wine from the Trapani province of western Sicily, produced in styles ranging from Fine to Vergine Riserva. Founded by British merchant John Woodhouse in 1796, the great historic houses include Cantine Florio (1833), Cantine Pellegrino (1880) and Marco De Bartoli, who revived artisan Marsala with his un-fortified Vecchio Samperi.

faqs.question: When is the best time to visit Sicily for wine tourism?
faqs.answer: The Etna harvest begins in October, often the latest in Italy due to the high altitude - a dramatic time to visit the volcano. September is harvest season for most lowland varieties. Spring (April to June) offers ideal hiking conditions in Etna's vineyards. Summer visits to Pantelleria and the Aeolian Islands combine wine and beach. Most wineries are open year-round with advance reservations, except island estates which operate seasonally.

faqs.question: What are the key wine sub-zones within Etna DOC?
faqs.answer: Etna DOC covers the slopes of Mount Etna across multiple communes. The north slope (Castiglione di Sicilia, Randazzo, Linguaglossa) is the most prestigious zone for Nerello Mascalese reds, with individual contrade (Calderara, Guardiola, Arcuria, San Lorenzo, Feudo di Mezzo) increasingly bottled separately as single-vineyard wines. Milo on the east slope is the exclusive zone for Etna Bianco Superiore from Carricante.
